seo.title: EV Charger Discovery | Neel
seo.description: A case study on designing a charger discovery experience around trust, availability, and faster decision-making for urban EV users.

sections.title: EV Charger Discovery
summary: This concept reframes charger discovery for gig workers and urban EV users as a trust-and-timing problem.
Instead of only helping users find a station, it helps them judge whether that station is worth committing to in the first place.

media.alt: Preview of the EV Charger Discovery mobile interface showing charger cards and a map-driven layout.

items.label: Problem
items.value: Nearby chargers can still be the wrong choice when queues, reliability, and wait conditions are unclear.

items.label: Approach
items.value: Explored a map-first interface that foregrounds queue status, station credibility, and decision confidence.

items.label: Outcome
items.value: The result is a clearer discovery layer built around trust, scan speed, and lower decision friction.

notes.title: Field note
notes.text: Had to spend an hour at a charging station because of the long queues.

notes.title: Service friction
notes.text: The first charger was broken, so was the second. I had to drive around to find one that was working.

notes.title: Breakdown moment
notes.text: The battery swapping station was under maintenance, the next station was 2kms away.

sections.label: Context
sections.heading: Charging decisions break down when uncertainty stacks up
sections.aside: The challenge is not only locating a charger, but deciding whether it is reliable enough to justify the detour. That uncertainty hits hardest for time-sensitive users whose routines depend on predictable charging.

sections.heading: Three user groups, three charging realities.

users.title: Urban Commuter
users.needs: Nearby charger status and viable alternatives

users.title: Fleet Owner
users.pains: Fleet health visibility and scheduling complexity

cards.title:
Prioritize
Trust Signals
cards.description: Queue visibility, charger status, and reliability cues were treated as core UI, not secondary metadata.

cards.title:
Reduce
Scan Time
cards.description: The hierarchy helped users compare stations fast, without having to drill into every option.

cards.title: Support Faster Commitment
cards.description: The interaction model was built to make route tradeoffs and queue risk legible before arrival.

image.alt: Rendered interface of the EV Charger Discovery concept.

metrics.label: Trust
metrics.text: Reliability cues move earlier in the flow.

metrics.label: Scan time
metrics.text: Nearby stations become faster to compare.

metrics.label: Queue
metrics.text: Wait conditions are easier to read before arrival.
